summary: My name is Kiyoshi Noji, born in 1899 in a village north of Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. I was raised in a farming family with samurai ancestry and became deeply involved in Christian ministry, attending Tohoku Gakuin University and later studying theology in the United States at the Pacific School of Religion and Princeton University. My journey to America in 1926 was inspired by my cousin Rev. Kikuchi, and I pursued my calling as a Christian minister despite early hardships and cultural adjustments. I have fond memories of my education and the Christian community that supported me both in Japan and the U.S.

I was born in Miyagi Prefecture, in a small village north of Sendai city. This rural area was where my family farmed and where I spent my childhood, surrounded by the traditions of my samurai ancestors and the Buddhist faith of my family. Miyagi Prefecture shaped my early life experiences, including my education at local schools before I moved on to Tohoku Gakuin in Sendai.

Sendai is the city near where I was born and raised in Miyagi Prefecture. It was in Sendai that I attended Tohoku Gakuin University, a Christian mission school, completing high school, college, and part of theology school there. Sendai was central to my early education and spiritual development before I came to the United States.

I attended Tohoku Gakuin University in Sendai, a Christian mission school where I completed high school, college, and one year of theology school. The school had a strong American influence, with many teachers and visitors from the U.S., which helped me learn about America and inspired me to pursue ministry. It was there that I studied English and literature, including Shakespeare, and prepared for my future studies abroad.

Japan is my homeland where I was born and raised in Miyagi Prefecture. I grew up during the Meiji era and witnessed events like the Russo-Japanese War, which left strong impressions on me. My family background was rooted in samurai tradition and Zen Buddhism, but I embraced Christianity during my youth. Japan shaped my early life and education before I moved to the United States to continue my theological studies.

After coming to America in 1926, I enrolled at the Pacific School of Religion where I earned my Bachelor of Divinity. This school was a key part of my theological education in the United States, and it helped me improve my English and adapt to American culture. I stayed in Berkeley, California during this time and appreciated the supportive Christian community there.

Following my studies at the Pacific School of Religion, I attended Princeton University for postgraduate work and received a Master's degree. At Princeton, I was one of several Japanese students, including Katsukei Nakamura, and experienced American academic life in the late 1920s and early 1930s. My time there was marked by cultural exchanges and some humorous incidents, though I was focused on my studies and ministry calling.

My family followed the Zenshū Buddhist tradition, and my ancestors were closely connected to Zenshū priests. Although I was drawn to Christianity, I often attended Zenshū sermons with my father and grandfather during my childhood. This religious background influenced my early spiritual life before I fully embraced Christianity.

My ancestors were samurai until the time of my grandfather, but after the samurai class was abolished, my family became farmers. This samurai heritage was part of my family's identity, and my father was raised with the old samurai values, which made him a strict man. Although I moved away from that tradition, it remained a significant part of my family's history.

I had an uncle who served in the navy and was stationed at Yokosuka Naval Base. He was influenced by Christian sermons he heard there and encouraged our family to consider Christianity. His experiences and faith played a role in my own journey toward becoming a Christian minister.

Southern California is where my cousin, Rev. Kikuchi, now lives in retirement. He was an important figure in my life, as his home was a meeting place for Christians in our area back in Japan, and he had already established himself as a pastor at Wintersburg Church when I arrived in America. Southern California became a significant location for my early years in the U.S.

Rev. Kikuchi is my cousin who played a pivotal role in my life and ministry. He graduated from theology school in Japan, came to America before me, and served as pastor at Wintersburg Church in Southern California. His home was a Christian meeting place in our hometown, and he encouraged me to pursue my calling and supported me when I arrived in the United States.

I was born during the Meiji era, and I have memories from that time, such as sending off soldiers with my mother around Meiji 37 (1904). The era was marked by Japan's modernization and military conflicts like the Russo-Japanese War, which affected my family and community. These events shaped the environment of my childhood.

I remember the Russo-Japanese War vividly, especially the victory parade after the war ended. My uncle returned from the war, and the government requisitioned horses from farmers, including ours, to support the military effort. These events left a strong impression on me as a child growing up in rural Japan.

In my senior year of high school at Tohoku Gakuin, I was sent as a delegate to a Christian Young Men's Conference held in Gotemba, near Mount Fuji. It was the first time I left my hometown, and I was deeply impressed by the speakers and the experience. This event was a milestone in my spiritual and personal growth.

Mount Fuji was near the location of the Christian Young Men's Conference I attended in Gotemba during my senior year of high school. Although I did not describe much about the mountain itself, the conference near this iconic place was a memorable experience for me as I left my hometown for the first time.

During my studies at Tohoku Gakuin, I read Shakespeare and other famous literature translated into Japanese. These works impressed me greatly and were part of my liberal arts education that prepared me for theology school. Shakespeare's literature broadened my understanding of language and culture.

I came to the United States in 1926 to continue my theological education, following my cousin Rev. Kikuchi who was already a pastor here. I studied at the Pacific School of Religion in Berkeley and later at Princeton University. The U.S. was a place of opportunity and challenge, where I experienced cultural differences but was supported by the Christian community.

Wintersburg Church in Southern California was where my cousin Rev. Kikuchi served as pastor when I arrived in America. I stayed at his place before starting school, and the church was a hub for the Japanese Christian community in the area. It was important for my transition to life in the United States.

When I arrived in San Francisco, Mr. Tomizawa, the secretary of the YMCA and a fellow Tohoku Gakuin alumnus, was the first person to meet me. He helped me navigate the immigration process and introduced me to other alumni in California. The YMCA played a supportive role in my early days in America.

I lived in Berkeley, California while attending the Pacific School of Religion. The dormitory there was welcoming, and I was treated well despite some anti-Japanese sentiments in the wider community. Berkeley was where I grew academically and socially during my early years in America.

I recall the Oakland Japanese Methodist Church as part of the Japanese Christian community in California. I heard about incidents of discrimination there, such as Miss Yukiko Furuta, a Japanese student who was denied the honor of valedictorian despite her excellent grades. This church and community were part of the social fabric for Japanese Americans during my time.

Although my transcript does not detail my personal experiences during the Japanese American internment, I mentioned that after the war broke out, many shocking incidents occurred. The internment was a significant and painful event for the Japanese American community, and I was aware of its impact during and after World War II.

At Princeton University, I met Katsukei Nakamura, a Japanese student and commando in the Japanese Navy who was married to General Togo's niece. He was sent by the Navy Department to study in the U.S., and we were part of a small group of Japanese students there. His presence highlighted the connections between Japan's military and educational efforts abroad.

My uncle served in the Imperial Japanese Navy, and I also knew Katsukei Nakamura, a commando in the Navy studying at Princeton. The Navy influenced my family and acquaintances, and it played a role in the broader context of Japan's military during my youth and early adulthood. The Navy's presence was felt in my community and educational circles.

General Tōgō Heihachirō was a prominent figure connected to my acquaintance Katsukei Nakamura, who was married to his niece. Tōgō was a celebrated naval hero from the Russo-Japanese War era, and his legacy was well known in my family and among those connected to the Navy. His influence was part of the military heritage that shaped my environment growing up.

The transcript does not provide specific details about my interactions with the Japanese Embassy in Washington, D.C., but as a Japanese student and Christian minister in America during the early 20th century, I was aware of the diplomatic presence. The embassy represented Japan's interests in the U.S. during a time of complex relations between the two countries.
The transcript does not mention any personal experiences or connections with Booker T. Washington. Therefore, I cannot provide a summary about him based on my oral history.
